items.q: What is the difference between constructive and destructive waves?
items.correct: Constructive waves have strong swash and weak backwash, building beaches; destructive waves have weak swash and strong backwash, dragging sediment offshore and eroding cliffs.
items.misconception: Big waves are destructive and small waves are constructive.
items.random_wrong: Constructive waves build sandcastles and destructive waves wash them away.
items.why: It's the swash-to-backwash balance, not size, that classifies waves. Tall, plunging waves are destructive; long, spilling waves are constructive. Wave frequency also matters.

items.q: What is the difference between biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem?
items.correct: Biotic components are all living organisms (plants, animals, microbes); abiotic components are non-living factors (climate, soil, water, light, rock).
items.misconception: Biotic means animals and abiotic means plants.
items.random_wrong: Biotic and abiotic both refer to types of soil.
items.why: Plants are biotic, not abiotic. Students sort by familiarity rather than living vs non-living. The distinction matters because abiotic factors set the conditions for which biotic communities develop.

items.q: What is the difference between a megacity and a world city?
items.correct: A megacity is defined by population (over 10 million); a world city is defined by global economic and cultural influence (e.g. New York, London) — so a city can be one without the other.
items.misconception: Megacities and world cities are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A megacity has over 1 million people and a world city has over 5 million.
items.why: Dhaka and Kinshasa are megacities but not world cities; Zurich is a world city but not a megacity. The distinction is quantitative (size) vs qualitative (function and global connections).

items.correct: Electricity network balancing supply and use
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Electricity network balancing supply and use. “A type of road” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: More public transport. “More private cars” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many days a week is car-free Sunday in some cities?
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
